Questions¶

  1. user warning: c argument looks like a single numeric RGB or RGBA sequence, which should be avoided as value-mapping will have precedence in case its length matches with x & y. Please use the color keyword-argument or provide a 2-D array with a single row if you intend to specify the same RGB or RGBA value for all points.

j'ai mis les arguments "color", "colors", "color_palette" et "c", le tSNE-Visualizer soit les ignore, soit lève une erreur. Donc le programme marche correctement, mais il lève toujours cet avertissment.

  1. Section "Visualization with t-SNE calculation and scatterplot": ValueError: 'init' must be 'pca', 'random', or a numpy array. Ici on parle de quel tableau ? (si "numpy array" s'appelle bien un tableau en français" ça peut être par exemple quoi? Ce tableau peut venir d'où?

  2. La nouvelle question et la plus importante: IN 125: error: OpenCV(4.5.4) D:\a\opencv-python\opencv-python\opencv\modules\core\src\alloc.cpp:73: error: (-4:Insufficient memory) Failed to allocate 1498885248 bytes in function 'cv::OutOfMemoryError'

je pense qu'il n'a pas assez de memoir pour executer à la fois Word2Vec (qui demande le telechargement de base de vectoirs du poids 1.5 gigas) et calcul de descripteurs SIFT. Quand je couvre tout le bloc Word2Vec en commentaire, le code en IN 125 (calcul de descripteurs SIFT) s'execute sans soucis.

Pour solution, j'ai tésté de supprimer la variable associé avec la base de vectors (la variable qui s'appelle wv) et les variables qui utilisent cette variables (les fonctions "mean_word_vectors" et "word2vec_pipeline". Mais ça n'a pas aidé. Toujours le même erreur s'elève.

Y a-t-il un moyen de supprimer de mémoire des variables ou autres morceaux de code que l'on n'utilise plus, pour libérer d'espace mémoire?

J'ai des captures d'ecran de ce qui se passe avec la mémoire pendant l'execution:

L'utilisation de mémoire n'est pas constante: elle fait des "vagues" près d'image 300. Mais le programme survie cette vague: l'utilisation de mémoire rebaisse comme avant la "vague", et le programme continue s'executer. Puis près d'image 400 il y a une deuxième "vague": l'utilisation de mémoire monte. Mais cette fois le programme ne survie la vague que à condition que je n'avais pas encore executé avant le Word2Vec.

Load the database¶

Extraction of general category from column "product_category_tree"¶

Before I get my own labels from clustering (by KMeans or other methods) I can use the "general category" as labels.

In [9]:
# descri.product_category_tree[0]
# descri.product_category_tree[0].strip('[""]')
# descri.product_category_tree[0].strip('[""]').split(' >> ')
descri['full_category'] = descri.product_category_tree.str.strip('[""]').str.split(' >> ')
descri['full_category'][0][0]
Out[9]:
'Home Furnishing'
In [10]:
# descri['general_category'] = descri['full_category'].apply(lambda x: x[0])
descri['general_category'] = descri['full_category'].str.get(0)
descri.general_category.value_counts()
Out[10]:

Baby Care                     150
Watches                       150
Home Decor & Festive Needs    150
Kitchen & Dining              150
Beauty and Personal Care      150
Computers                     150

Vu que le nombre d'objets est le même partout, dans chaque catégorie, je suppose que cet echantillon était sélectionné comme ça exprès. Donc dans la classification il y a du sens dans l'approche à se cibler à 7 catégories qui se rapproches à les categories qu'on a extrait.

In [11]:
labels_names = list(descri.general_category.unique())
descri['label'] = 0

for name, i in zip(labels_names, range(7)):
    descri.loc[descri.general_category == name, 'label'] = i
    
# descri['sub_category'] = descri['full_category'].apply(lambda x: x[1])
descri['sub_category'] = descri['full_category'].str.get(1)

Image classification¶

It doesn't matter for the outcome of the project whether you treat images first or text first. Here I started with image treatment in order to handle one image with size 8484 * 11042 pixels. It works fine when I treat the images before text, with its Word2Vec vectors base of 1.6 Gb of memory uptaken.

To install the package cv2 (it's the same that opencv) with conda run: conda install -c menpo opencv3

Install cv2 through conda prompt is a bad idea, because the latest version which is available through Conda prompt is 4.0.1, which is under patent. The latest of all existing versions is 4.5.4, which is no more under patent. That patent has been expired in March (or July) 2020. To install the version 4.5.4, use pip command through Jupyter notebook.

pip install opencv-python

Requirement already satisfied: opencv-python in c:\users\veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages (4.5.4.60) Requirement already satisfied: numpy>=1.17.3 in c:\users\veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages (from opencv-python) (1.20.3) Note: you may need to restart the kernel to use updated packages.

pip install opencv-contrib-python

Requirement already satisfied: opencv-contrib-python in c:\users\veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages (4.5.4.60) Requirement already satisfied: numpy>=1.17.3 in c:\users\veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages (from opencv-contrib-python) (1.20.3) Note: you may need to restart the kernel to use updated packages.

In [13]:
def list_fct(name) :
    return list(descri[descri.general_category == name].image.values)

# list_kitchen = list_fct('Kitchen & Dining')
# list_beauty = list_fct('Beauty and Personal Care')
# list_computers = list_fct('Computers')


for name in labels_names:
    print(name)
    for i in range(3):
        plt.subplot(130 + 1 + i)
        filename = path_images + list_fct(name)[i+20]
#         print(filename)
        image = imread(filename)
        plt.imshow(image)
    plt.show()

Home Furnishing

Baby Care

Watches

Home Decor & Festive Needs

Kitchen & Dining

Beauty and Personal Care

Computers

Vectorization through SIFT¶

Vectorization through SIFT descriptors includes several steps:

  • Before searching for descriptors, every image must be converted in gray and equalized(in other words, "smoothed")
  • Create SIFT descriptors list for every image
  • Add all of them to the list of all descriptors of all images. Unlike the words of the language, it's highly unlikely to have two identical SIFT descriptors, so useless to convert the list to the set: the length of the the set of all the descriptors of all the images will not be any shorter than just sum of individual lengths
  • Group these SIFT descriptors with KMeans, or with MiniBatch KMeans to save time
  • Each cluster is considered a "visual word". Since this step we work with image much alike as with the text. We do it in a way that any descriptor belonging to a cluster N, is considered as a visual word "N". That means, all the descriptors from the same cluster are considered as one single word "N" without differentiation.
  • And every image is thus considered as a "bag of visual words", or "histograms". It's a vector showing how many times every cluster/"visual word" occurs in every image/"bag of visual words"/"histogram".
  • And then we do second clustering. This time maybe it's ok with KMeans, as we have a much fewer samples (though much higher dimensionality, equal to number of our clusters, or "vocabulary" of "visual words". No need to opt for MiniBatchKmenas to speed up the clustering.
  • Evaluation and visualization. As we have no parameters to compare, no need to create a comparative DataFrame. We can go straight to visualization.

Example / illustration of a principle: find out and display SIFT descriptors of an image¶

In [14]:
# sift = cv2.xfeatures2d.SIFT_create()
sift = cv2.SIFT_create()
image = cv2.imread(descri.image_fullpath[1],0) # convert in gray
image = cv2.equalizeHist(image)   # equalize image histogram
kp, desc = sift.detectAndCompute(image, None)
img=cv2.drawKeypoints(image, kp, image)
plt.imshow(img)
plt.show()
print(desc.shape[0], 'descriptors, each of them has a length of', desc.shape[1])

47651 descriptors, each of them has a length of 128

SIFT descriptors of all images¶

  • Before seraching for descriptors - each image must be converted to grey and equalized;
  • List of descriptors for every image - to be used for histogram/"bag of visual words" list creation;
  • List of all descriptors, for all images - to be used in clustering.

Should I resize the images before making them grey and equalize? That could help with overloading the memory. There is a very large image at line 677. Its size is 8484 * 11042 pixels.

But as we see later, the quality of classification with SIFT is poor even without resizing. So for not rising question whether the poor result could be caused by wrong resizing, I just don't do the resizing to show that even the full images don't allow a good classification.

In [15]:
# descri[descri.image == '5518124b75d6c6dfee4c2e4c0cfa716a.jpg']
In [16]:
# identification of key points and associated descriptors
sift_keypoints = []
time1=time.time()
# sift = cv2.xfeatures2d.SIFT_create(500)

# sift = cv2.SIFT_create(500)
my_model = cv2.SIFT_create(500)

# for image_num in range(1050) :
for image_num in range(descri.shape[0]) :
    if image_num%100 == 0 : print(image_num, end='  ')
#     image = cv2.imread(descri.loc[image_num, 'image_fullpath'], 0) # convert in gray
    image = cv2.imread(descri.image_fullpath[image_num], 0) # convert in gray
    # image = cv2.cvtColor(image,cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
    image = cv2.equalizeHist(image)   # equalize image histogram
    kp, sift_signi_poi = my_model.detectAndCompute(image, None)
    sift_keypoints.append(sift_signi_poi)

duration1 = time.time()-time1
print('Time for creation of all SIFT descriptors : ', '%1.2f' % duration1, 'seconds')

0  100  200  300  400  500  600  700  800  900  1000  Time for creation of all SIFT descriptors :  599.07 seconds

print(len(sift_keypoints))
test = [len(x) for x in sift_keypoints]
print(test[:10])

elements in the list "sift keypoints" don't have equal length. Which should be 500, I suppose. Some are longer than 500,
some are shorter. That's why if I convert this list to a numpy array, I can use only object dtype, not float.

In [18]:
# np.stack(np.asarray(sift_keypoints)) # ValueError: all input arrays must have the same shape

# sift_keypoints_by_img = np.array(sift_keypoints, dtype=float)
# sift_keypoints_by_img = np.asarray(sift_keypoints, dtype=float)
sift_keypoints_by_img = np.asarray(sift_keypoints, dtype=object)
sift_keypoints_all    = np.concatenate(sift_keypoints_by_img, axis=0) # shape (517351, 128)

print('Totally we have', sift_keypoints_all.shape[0], 'descriptors, each has the length', sift_keypoints_all.shape[1])

Totally we have 517351 descriptors, each has the length 128

Create the "bag of visual words"¶

What's a visual word: it's a cluster of descriptors. We bring the descriptors to groups, using KMeans, or minibatch KMeans, or Gaussian mixture, or another algorithm. One "visual word" can be long (many descriptors), another can be short (1-2 descriptors, kind of a n outlier in the clustering).

Why create the "bag of visual words": because we need to describe an image as a vector with X coordinates where X is the same for every image. And the number of descriptor is not the same for every image, it could vary from less than 300 to more than 600 even when we set the precise quantity of descriptors (500 in our case).

Why else create the "bag of visual words" : for reducing the dimentionality of vocabulary. The size of vocabulary is 517,351 descriptors. I believe it's a sparce matrix, so maybe we could use Truncated SVD or SparcePca on it?) If we assign this vocabulary to the documents set, it will be either a 3D matrix, where to every document correspond a 2D (sparce) matrix of 517,351 * 128. Or if we flatten that 2D matrix, we obtain the vector of length 66,220,928 for every document.

So we pass from the space "coordinates of a descriptor(128) number of all the descriptors for all the images (517,351)" to the space "arbitrarily chosen number of "visual words" (from 10 n_clusters = 10 * 7 = 70 to sqrt of 517,351 = 720)

In [19]:
OMP_NUM_THREADS=4

# Determination number of clusters
time1=time.time()

k = int(np.sqrt(len(sift_keypoints_all)))
print('How many clusters/"visual words" do we have:', k)
print(k, 'clusters/"visual words" are being created...')

# Clustering
kmeans = cluster.MiniBatchKMeans(n_clusters=k, init_size=3*k, batch_size=2050)
kmeans.fit(sift_keypoints_all)

duration1 = time.time()-time1
print(k, 'clusters/"visual words" are created in', '%1.2f' % duration1, 'seconds')

How many clusters/"visual words" do we have: 719
719 clusters/"visual words" are being created...
719 clusters/"visual words" are created in 13.16 seconds

C:\Users\Veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\sklearn\cluster_kmeans.py:1046: UserWarning: MiniBatchKMeans is known to have a memory leak on Windows with MKL, when there are less chunks than available threads. You can prevent it by setting batch_size >= 2048 or by setting the environment variable OMP_NUM_THREADS=4

Every cluster is a "visual word", all together all the clusters are th "bag of visual words".

Now let's see how often each "word"(cluster) occurs in each "document"(image). We do an analog of CountVectorizer.

I suppose we don't have to divise by occurence of the cluster in the whole set of documents, to make an analogy of TfIdf Vectorizer, because these clusters are more unique than words of language (or not?)

In [20]:
# Creation of histograms (features)
time1 = time.time()

def build_histogram(kmeans, descriptors, image_num):
    k = len(kmeans.cluster_centers_) # array length k defined in previous cell
    res = kmeans.predict(descriptors)
    hist = np.zeros(k) # array length k defined in previous cell
    nb_des = len(descriptors)
    if nb_des == 0 : print('No descriptors found for image #', image_num)
    for i in res:
        hist[i] += 1/nb_des
    return hist


# Creation of a matrix of histograms
hist_vectors=[]

for i, image_desc in enumerate(sift_keypoints_by_img) :
    if i % 100 == 0 : print(i, end=' ')  
    hist = build_histogram(kmeans, image_desc, i) #calculates the histogram
    hist_vectors.append(hist) #histogram is the feature vector

SIFT_features = np.asarray(hist_vectors)

duration1=time.time()-time1
print('Histograms are created in ', '%1.2f' % duration1, 'seconds')

0 100 200 300 400 500 600 700 800 900 1000 Histograms are created in  54.34 seconds

Dimensionality reduction¶

Here we do the pre-reduction with PCA, because tSNE becomes slow with higher dimensionality, so it's better to do a pre-reduction.

SIFT_pca = feat_pca

In [22]:
print('Dimensions before PCA : ', SIFT_features.shape)
pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)
SIFT_pca = pca.fit_transform(SIFT_features)
print('Dimensions after PCA : ', SIFT_features.shape)

tsne = manifold.TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=20, learning_rate='auto',
                     n_iter=2000, init='random')


# X_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(trans_arr) # max ARI 0.0, usually 0.02-0.03
SIFT_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(SIFT_features) # max ARI 0.07, usually 0.04-0.05

df_SIFT = pd.DataFrame(SIFT_tsne[:, 0:2], columns=['tsne1', 'tsne2'])
print(df_SIFT.shape)

Dimensions before PCA :  (1050, 719)
Dimensions after PCA :  (1050, 719)
(1050, 2)
In [23]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=orig_labels_names,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_SIFT,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of ground truth labels', fontsize=20, pad=35, fontweight='bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 

plt.show()

Looks like the histograms don't reveal any structure. So, the very best we can hope is still quite close to 0. Let's check this hypothesis: try to make a clustering and evaluate with ground truth labels (ARI etc) and without them (Silhouette etc).

Clusters¶

Based on PCA projection¶

In [24]:
kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(SIFT_pca)

SIFT_pca_labels = kmeans.labels_
print(df_SIFT.shape)

(1050, 2)
In [26]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=SIFT_pca_labels,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_SIFT,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of KMeans on PCA projection', fontsize=20, pad=35, fontweight='bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 
plt.show()

print('ARI :', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, SIFT_pca_labels))

ARI : 0.03497749191222039

5      1
dtype: int64

print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, SIFT_pca_labels))

print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(SIFT_pca, SIFT_pca_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

df_cm = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat, index = [label for label in labels_names])

Based on t-SNE projection¶

In [48]:
kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(SIFT_tsne)

SIFT_tsne_labels = kmeans.labels_
print(df_SIFT.shape)

(1050, 2)
In [61]:

    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=SIFT_tsne_labels,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_SIFT,
    legend='brief')

# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')

Pre-trained VGG16 with all layers (including classifier of 1000 classes)¶

In [72]:
vgg16_model = VGG16()
vgg16_model
Out[72]:
<tensorflow.python.keras.engine.functional.Functional at 0x2670bbf7a00>

Example of 1 image converted to vector¶

Thie following code works. I commented it out because it's useless for the treatment of the data

In [73]:
# img0 = load_img(path_images + '00e966a5049a262cfc72e6bbf68b80e7.jpg', target_size=(224, 224))  # Charger l'image
# img0

# img1 = img_to_array(img0)  # Convertir en tableau numpy
# print(img1.dtype, img1.shape)
# img1[0][:3]

# img2 = img1.reshape((1, img1.shape[0], img1.shape[1], img1.shape[2]))
# print(img2.dtype, img2.shape)
# img2[0][0][:3]

# img3 = preprocess_input(img2)
# print(img2.dtype, img2.shape)
# img3[0][0][:3]
In [74]:
img = load_img(path_images + '0a3b5fdf77a361c2d7d9b29c259b8c4e.jpg', target_size=(224, 224))  # Load the image
display(img)
img = img_to_array(img)  # Convertir en tableau numpy
img = img.reshape((1, img.shape[0], img.shape[1], img.shape[2]))  # Make a collection of images (1 sample, 1 image)
img = preprocess_input(img)  # Preprocess the image as VGG-16 wants

img = vgg16_model.predict(img)  # Predict image class (of 1000 classes of ImageNet)
# The result is a vector: a list of length 1000, each coordinate is a probability for a given class
# On obtient le résultat du réseau, c'est-à-dire un vector, une liste de 1000 probabilités.
In [75]:
# Afficher les 3 classes les plus probables
print('Top 3 :', decode_predictions(img, top=3)[0])

Top 3 : [('n04235860', 'sleeping_bag', 0.97583437), ('n04209133', 'shower_cap', 0.014998215), ('n04370456', 'sweatshirt', 0.0019061128)]

Apply the procedure to all images, convert to vectors of 1000 classes probabilities¶

In [76]:
test1 = descri.image_fullpath.map(lambda x: load_img(x, target_size=(224, 224)))
print(type(test1), test1.dtype) # test1[i] doesn't have a shape, it's an image
display(test1[677]) # this image originally had size 8484 * 11,042 pixels

C:\Users\Veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\PIL\Image.py:2918: DecompressionBombWarning: Image size (93680328 pixels) exceeds limit of 89478485 pixels, could be decompression bomb DOS attack.
  warnings.warn(

<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'> object

C:\Users\Veronika\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\PIL\Image.py:2918: DecompressionBombWarning: Image size (93680328 pixels) exceeds limit of 89478485 pixels, could be decompression bomb DOS attack.

In [77]:
test1 = test1.map(lambda x: img_to_array(x))
print(type(test1), test1.dtype, 'shape', test1[0].shape)
test1[0][0][:2]

<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'> object shape (224, 224, 3)
Out[77]:
array([[206., 204., 192.],
       [205., 203., 191.]], dtype=float32)
In [78]:
test2 = test1.map(lambda x: x.reshape((1, x.shape[0], x.shape[1], x.shape[2]))) # Make a collection of images (1 sample, 1 image)
print(type(test2), test2.dtype, 'shape', test2[0].shape)
test2[0][0][0][:3]

<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'> object shape (1, 224, 224, 3)
Out[78]:
array([[206., 204., 192.],
       [205., 203., 191.],
       [205., 202., 193.]], dtype=float32)
In [79]:
test3 = test2.map(lambda x: preprocess_input(x))
print(type(test2), test2.dtype, 'shape', test2[0].shape)
test3[0][0][0][:3]

<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'> object shape (1, 224, 224, 3)
Out[79]:
array([[88.061, 87.221, 82.32 ],
       [87.061, 86.221, 81.32 ],
       [89.061, 85.221, 81.32 ]], dtype=float32)
In [80]:
time1 = time.time()
test4 = test3.map(lambda x: vgg16_model.predict(x))

print(f'prediction of 1000 probabilities for 1050 images is done in {time.time() - time1} seconds.')

prediction of 1000 probabilities for 1050 images is done in 314.95488929748535 seconds.
In [81]:
test4 = test4.map(lambda x: x[0]) #remove the "envelop" of the array; convert a collection to a single image
In [82]:
VGG16_probabilities = np.stack(test4.values)
VGG16_probabilities.shape
Out[82]:
(1050, 1000)

Dimentionality reduction with PCA on VGG16_probabilities¶

In [83]:
pca = PCA(n_components=0.99)
In [84]:
vgg16_full_trans = pca.fit_transform(VGG16_probabilities)
vgg16_full_trans.shape
Out[84]:
(1050, 202)
In [86]:
# clustering with KMeans on image array
kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(vgg16_full_trans) 
VGG16_full_pca_labels = kmeans.labels_

kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(VGG16_probabilities) # ARI or silhouette ar both merely the same that with PCA, actually even a bit worse
VGG16_full_labels = kmeans.labels_
In [93]:
print('ARI of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('Homogeneity score of KMeans labels', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('Completeness score of KMeans labels', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('Silhouette score of KMeans labels', metrics.silhouette_score(vgg16_full_trans, VGG16_full_pca_labels))
print('Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(vgg16_full_trans, VGG16_full_pca_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(vgg16_full_trans, VGG16_full_pca_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

Dimentionality reduction with tSNE on VGG16_probabilities¶

In [126]:
tsne = manifold.TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=20, 
                     n_iter=2000, init='random', learning_rate='auto')

vgg16_full_trans_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(vgg16_full_trans)
df_VGG_full = pd.DataFrame(vgg16_full_trans_tsne[:,0:2], columns=['tsne1', 'tsne2'])

# X_tsne_full = tsne.fit_transform(VGG16_probabilities) # the ARI isn't any better
# df_VGG_full = pd.DataFrame(X_tsne_full[:,0:2], columns=['tsne1', 'tsne2'])

print(df_VGG_full.shape)

(1050, 2)
In [127]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=orig_labels,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_VGG_full,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of ground truth labels', fontsize = 20, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 

plt.show()
In [128]:

(1050, 3)

print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(vgg16_full_trans_tsne, VGG16_full_tsne_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue='tsne_labels',
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_VGG_full,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of tSNE labels', fontsize = 20, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 

plt.show()

The result of the image clustering is poorer than that of text treatment.

Between SIFT and VGG16, VGG 16 gives better metrics.

Pre-trained VGG16 with all layers but last one (without the classifier)¶

This approach allows to recover the features - the vectors of 4096 values. Then the approach is the same: dimensionality reduction (this can be done with PCA, TSNE, or something else (maybe NMF for positive values), clustering, evaluation.

In [133]:
# vgg16_model = VGG16()
my_model = models.Sequential()

for layer in vgg16_model.layers[:-1]:
    my_model.add(layer)    

# Freeze the layers 
for layer in my_model.layers:
    layer.trainable = False

# # Add 'softmax' instead of earlier 'prediction' layer
# # THIS WE CAN DO WITH CLASSIFICATION BUT NOT WITH A CLUSTERING
# my_model.add(Dense(7, activation='softmax'))
In [134]:
time1 = time.time()
test5 = test3.map(lambda x: my_model.predict(x))

print(f'prediction of 4096 features for 1050 images is done in {time.time() - time1} seconds.')

prediction of 4096 features for 1050 images is done in 299.21278166770935 seconds.
In [135]:
test5 = test5.map(lambda x: x[0]) #remove the "envelop" of the array; convert a collection to a single image
In [136]:
VGG16_features = np.stack(test5.values)
VGG16_features.shape
Out[136]:
(1050, 4096)

Dimentionality reduction with PCA on VGG16_features¶

In [137]:
pca = PCA(n_components=0.99)
In [138]:
vgg16_part_trans = pca.fit_transform(VGG16_features)
vgg16_part_trans.shape
Out[138]:
(1050, 803)
In [139]:
# clustering with KMeans on image array
kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(vgg16_part_trans) 
VGG16_part_pca_labels = kmeans.labels_

kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(VGG16_features) # ARI or silhouette ar both merely the same that with PCA, actually even a bit worse
VGG16_part_labels = kmeans.labels_
In [141]:
conf_mat = metrics.confusion_matrix(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels)
print('ARI of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('Homogeneity score of KMeans labels', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('Completeness score of KMeans labels', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('Silhouette score of KMeans labels', metrics.silhouette_score(vgg16_part_trans, VGG16_part_pca_labels))
print('Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(vgg16_part_trans, VGG16_part_pca_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(vgg16_part_trans, VGG16_part_pca_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

Completeness score of KMeans labels 0.6244052208339724

In [142]:

plt.figure()
ax3 = sns.heatmap(df_cm, annot=True, cmap='Blues', fmt='g')

In [145]:
conf_mat = metrics.confusion_matrix(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels)
print('ARI of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels))
print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels))
print('Homogeneity score of KMeans labels', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels))
print('Completeness score of KMeans labels', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels))
print('Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_labels))
print('Silhouette score of KMeans labels', metrics.silhouette_score(VGG16_features, VGG16_part_labels))
print('Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(VGG16_features, VGG16_part_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(VGG16_features, VGG16_part_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

ARI of KMeans labels 0.4811041368993192
AMutual Info score of KMeans labels 0.5971047365818293
Homogeneity score of KMeans labels 0.5848370218220439
Completeness score of KMeans labels 0.6176583404132548
Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels 0.5645230846176914
Silhouette score of KMeans labels 0.08557789
Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels 3.0867774119779665
Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels 49.469435421535934
In [146]:
df_cm = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat, index = [label for label in labels_names]) 
plt.figure()
ax3 = sns.heatmap(df_cm, annot=True, cmap='Blues', fmt='g')
plt.show()

Dimentionality reduction with tSNE on VGG16_features¶

In [147]:
tsne = manifold.TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=20, 
                     n_iter=2000, init='random', learning_rate='auto')

vgg16_part_trans_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(vgg16_part_trans)
df_VGG_part = pd.DataFrame(vgg16_part_trans_tsne[:,0:2], columns=['tsne1', 'tsne2'])

# vgg16_part_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(VGG16_features) # the ARI isn't any better
# df_VGG_part = pd.DataFrame(vgg16_part_tsne[:,0:2], columns=['tsne1', 'tsne2'])

print(df_VGG_part.shape)

(1050, 2)
In [148]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=orig_labels,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_VGG_part,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of ground truth labels', fontsize = 20, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 

plt.show()
In [149]:
kmeans = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
kmeans.fit(vgg16_part_trans_tsne)

VGG16_part_tsne_labels = kmeans.labels_
df_VGG_part['tsne_labels'] = kmeans.labels_
print(df_VGG_part.shape)

(1050, 3)
In [151]:
conf_mat = metrics.confusion_matrix(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels)
print('ARI of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('Homogeneity score of KMeans labels', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('Completeness score of KMeans labels', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('Silhouette score of KMeans labels', metrics.silhouette_score(vgg16_part_trans_tsne, VGG16_part_tsne_labels))
print('Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(vgg16_part_trans_tsne, VGG16_part_tsne_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(vgg16_part_trans_tsne, VGG16_part_tsne_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

ARI of KMeans labels 0.45581113718512606
AMutual Info score of KMeans labels 0.5641726313677836

Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels 0.5357191154119432

In [152]:
df_cm = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat, index = [label for label in labels_names]) 
plt.figure()
ax3 = sns.heatmap(df_cm, annot=True, cmap='Blues', fmt='g')
plt.show()
In [153]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x='tsne1', y='tsne2',
    hue=VGG16_part_tsne_labels,
    palette=sns.color_palette('tab10', n_colors=7), s=20, alpha=0.4,
    data=df_VGG_part,
    legend='brief')

plt.title('TSNE of tSNE labels', fontsize = 20, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
# plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize=16, fontweight = 'bold')
plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 

plt.show()

The result of the image clustering is poorer than that of text treatment.

Between SIFT and VGG16, VGG 16 gives better metrics. Inside VGG16, features recovery is better than probabilities recovery. As for dimensionality reduction, both PCA and tSNE work equally well. tSNE works a little bit better: ARI 0.48 while with PCA ARI 0.46.

Text treatment¶

Remove of "rubbish text"¶

Many descriptions contain "rubbish text", that can't help identify the category of the product. I could identify only: (maybe there are more)

  • '- Great Discounts '
  • 'Only Genuine Products'
  • '30 Day Replacement Guarant'

I identified it during work in "text analysis" section (the one below): creating Text objects and running "concordance", "similar" and "common_contexts" methods with various words. You can see these tests below, after removal of (some of) the rubbish words.

In [154]:
# descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.apply(lambda x: x.lower())
descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.lower()
In [155]:
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('Only Genuine Products', case=False)].index # 564
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('Genuine Products', case=False)].index # not more, also 564
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('Great Discounts', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('at best prices', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('Lowest Prices', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('free shipping', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('cash on delivery', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('in India Only at Flipkart.com', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('Flipkart.com', case=False)].index # 384-226-150 = 8
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('best quality', case=False)].index 
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('online', case=False)].index
# descri[descri.description.str.contains('price', case=False)].index
In [156]:
# descri.loc[5, 'description']
# descri.loc[35, 'description']
# descri.loc[56, 'description']
# descri.loc[125, 'description']
# descri.loc[5, 'description']
# descri.loc[993, 'description']
# descri.loc[1049, 'description']

Meaningless words, specific to this document set, as "price", "buy" etc, can be thrown away with the parameter max_df in vectorizer, be that Count or Tfidf.

In [157]:
empty_phrases = ['great discounts,', 'genuine products', '30 day replacement guarantee', 'cash on delivery'
'free shipping',
'lowest prices,', 'best prices',
'in india only at flipkart.com', 'flipkart.com',
'key features', 'high quiality', 'best quiality',
'promise to deliver best quality products']
for empty_phrase in empty_phrases:
    descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.replace(empty_phrase,' ', regex=False)

https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.Series.str.replace.html

Break long words into short tokens. It would be handier to get used to re.sub() instead of replace.

This replacement avoids glueing of several words into one long token that will be removed as a hapax on next steps.

In [158]:
gluey_symbols = ['.', '_', '-', '/', ':', ',']
for symb in gluey_symbols:
    descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.replace(symb,' ', regex=False)
descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.replace('wi fi', 'wifi')

C:\Users\Veronika\AppData\Local\Temp/ipykernel_23292/2000182604.py:8: FutureWarning: The default value of regex will change from True to False in a future version. descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.replace(empty_phrase,' ')

C:\Users\Veronika\AppData\Local\Temp/ipykernel_23292/3150675927.py:3: FutureWarning: The default value of regex will change from True to False in a future version. In addition, single character regular expressions will not be treated as literal strings when regex=True. descri.loc[:, 'description'] = descri.description.str.replace(symb,' ')

Text analysis after removal of "rubbish text"¶

In [159]:
total_description = ' '.join(descri.description)
In [160]:
regexp = nltk.RegexpTokenizer(r'\w+')
wordpunct = nltk.WordPunctTokenizer() # (pattern='\\w+|[^\\w\\s]+', gaps=False, discard_empty=True, flags=re.UNICODE|re.MULTILINE|re.DOTALL)
In [161]:
# total_description = total_description.lower()
test_w = Text(nltk.word_tokenize(total_description))
test_regex = Text(regexp.tokenize(total_description))
test_wp = Text(wordpunct.tokenize(total_description))
print(len(test_w), len(test_regex), len(test_wp))

79187 76095 79540
In [162]:
# test_w.concordance('features') # genuine, India, rs. originally it's Rs which means indian rupies
# # print()
# # test_w.similar('good')
# # test_w.common_contexts(['good', 'your'])
# # test_w.common_contexts(['good', 'ideal'])
# # # test_w.dispersion_plot(['brilliant', 'extra', 'large', 'compact']) # the same for word_tokenize regexp and wordpunct
In [163]:
test_w.concordance('abkl') # genuine, India, rs. originally it's Rs which means indian rupies

ivery ! buy arsalan 10 5 inch glass hookah for rs 349 online arsalan 10 5 inch

bian nights soex cranberry assorted hookah flavor ( 500 g ) price rs 800 an ex
alls ech oly ceramic inside fitting hookah mouth tip ( black ) price rs 699 du
s of ech oly ceramic inside fitting hookah mouth tip ( black ) general brand e

of	to	in	rs	on	is	at	cm	it	's	be	or	no	as	by	an	''	ml	do	so	``	12	10	if	15	mm	id	we	19	up	5v	50	18	pc	40	75	9a	16	17	30	60	13	hp	20	14	45	ce	gb	90	ac	go	kg	34	80	me	32	fs	na	he	23	24	11	my	3d	01	48	22	35	tv	36	pl	ft	43	28	29	59	65	25	27	33	85	86	89	06	69	55	21	hz	56	sf	70	95	pk	re	uv	tr	us	co	fb	58	64	37	04	nx	38	72	88	mh	26	'm	78	62	tp	sd	cl	98	hd	i3	53	st	2w	68	ii	bk	br	iw	fk	sp	51	47	77	la	84	vj	aa	hg	gw	vc	rg	bw	yk	am	ki	bd	99	cc	02	°c	°f	49	42	94	83	dg	08	xp	sq	39	pg	ro	te	rt	ar	tl	gm	lp	lm	31	hi	03	sj	xl	pu	g6	cb	u3	gx	5s	5c	de	97	al	tc	46	v1	2g	±3	sw	ri	ic	ke	07	wo	os	kh	ma	v9	cd	ve	ts	eb	eq	lx	a5	3g	a6	wf	c1	td	rp	09	av	66	mn	xn	4d	ss	mx	md	tt	ta	cp	ez	mb	91	92	rb	ms	57	ca	nk	00	ha	oz
In [169]:
# for x in fdist_w:
#     if len(x) == 3:
#         print(x, end='\t')

# for x in fdist_regex:
#     if len(x) == 3:
#         print(x, end='\t')

# for x in fdist_wp:
#     if len(x) == 3:
#         print(x, end='\t')

Tokens with 2 symbols have some meaningful words, as ml, rs (Indian Rupies), go, kg, cm, tv, ft, hz, (wi, fi), cd, mm, oz.

Tokens with 1 or 2 letters can be removed. Tokens with 3 letters can not. They have some meaningful words, like "mug", "car", "boy", "dvd" or "gsm".

In [170]:
test_w = Text([tok for tok in test_w if len(tok) > 2])
test_regex = Text([tok for tok in test_regex if len(tok) > 2])
test_wp = Text([tok for tok in test_wp if len(tok) > 2])
In [171]:
test_w = Text([tok for tok in test_w if tok.isalpha()])
test_regex = Text([tok for tok in test_regex if tok.isalpha()])
test_wp = Text([tok for tok in test_wp if tok.isalpha()])
In [172]:
vocab_w = sorted(set(test_w))
# print(vocab_w[:9])
print(len(vocab_w))

vocab_regex = sorted(set(test_regex))
# print(vocab_regex[:9])
print(len(vocab_regex))

vocab_wp = sorted(set(test_wp))
# print(vocab_wp[:9])
print(len(vocab_wp))

4866
4876
4876
In [173]:
print(set(test_wp).difference(set(test_w)))
print(set(test_regex).difference(set(test_w)))
# print(set(test_wp).difference(set(test_regex))) # empty
# print(set(test_w).difference(set(test_regex))) # empty
# print(set(test_regex).difference(set(test_wp))) # empty
# print(set(test_w).difference(set(test_wp))) # empty

{'won', 'pipes', 'repouss', 'cor', 'pegs', 'techone', 'qur', 'apollo', 'signer', 'thnkss'}
{'won', 'pipes', 'repouss', 'cor', 'pegs', 'techone', 'qur', 'apollo', 'signer', 'thnkss'}

Tokens which are not digits (or are not words) can be removed.

In [174]:
# lexical diversity is vocab size / text size: close to 0 is repetitive vocab, close to 1 is diverse vocab
print(len(set(test_w)) / len(test_w))
print(len(set(test_regex)) / len(test_regex))
print(len(set(test_wp)) / len(test_wp))

0.08262301762488539
0.08271556769410847
0.08271556769410847
In [175]:
fdist_w = nltk.FreqDist(test_w)
fdist_regex = nltk.FreqDist(test_regex)
fdist_wp = nltk.FreqDist(test_wp)
In [176]:
print(fdist_w.most_common(8)) # after that, go some meaningful words , then again meaningless
print(fdist_regex.most_common(8)) # after that, go some meaningful words , then again meaningless
print(fdist_wp.most_common(50)) # after that, go some meaningful words , then again meaningless

[('for', 1447), ('the', 1369), ('and', 1332), ('with', 843), ('only', 756), ('your', 656), ('free', 618), ('buy', 581)]
[('for', 1447), ('the', 1369), ('and', 1332), ('with', 843), ('only', 756), ('your', 656), ('free', 618), ('buy', 581)]
[('for', 1447), ('the', 1369), ('and', 1332), ('with', 843), ('only', 756), ('your', 656), ('free', 618), ('buy', 581), ('delivery', 567), ('shipping', 564), ('cash', 564), ('this', 444), ('online', 396), ('from', 382), ('specifications', 371), ('you', 360), ('price', 352), ('watch', 330), ('color', 328), ('mug', 327), ('baby', 320), ('pack', 317), ('material', 312), ('set', 310), ('type', 304), ('cotton', 299), ('box', 292), ('general', 288), ('package', 269), ('analog', 262), ('sales', 261), ('number', 253), ('warranty', 250), ('quality', 242), ('details', 235), ('product', 233), ('brand', 232), ('model', 226), ('design', 225), ('ceramic', 219), ('are', 215), ('laptop', 214), ('fabric', 211), ('showpiece', 206), ('can', 198), ('made', 189), ('not', 189), ('features', 188), ('that', 185), ('perfect', 184)]

print(fdist_w['good']) # on the early stages of treatment the results were different for different tokenizers
# print(fdist_regex['good']) # after punctuation symbols removed, they are the same
# print(fdist_wp['good'])

40
In [178]:
# test_w.collocations()  # frequent bigrams
# test_regex.collocations(10)  # the lists of bigrams were different on early stages of treatment
test_wp.collocations(50) # after treatment they became identical

shipping cash; cash delivery; free shipping; sales package; analog
watch; baby girl; number contents; contents sales; delivery buy;
general brand; combo set; model name; baby boy; ceramic mug; package
pack; stainless steel; microwave safe; porcelain crafting; stays
permanent; style code; crafting ensuring; loved ones; print shapes;
high quality; thrilling yet; exclusive creation; fabric cotton; water
resistant; mouse pad; pizza cutter; fresh start; years come; creation
rockmantra; yet fresh; give thrilling; fabric care; day exclusive; box
number; covered warranty; general details; box sales; occasion casual;
laptop skin; features porcelain; model number; design stays; may vary;
pick gift; perfect pick; making perfect

It would be useful to remove meaningless collocations, like "key features" or "high quality".

Upd: I already did it in the section above.

In [179]:
long_words_w = [w for w in vocab_w if len(w) == 14]
# long_words_regex = [w for w in vocab_regex if len(w) == 14]
# long_words_wp = [w for w in vocab_wp if len(w) == 14]
print(sorted(long_words_w))
# print(sorted(long_words_regex))
# print(sorted(long_words_wp))

['authentication', 'breathlessness', 'brilliantcolor', 'certifications', 'characteristic', 'classification', 'disintegrating', 'electrotherapy', 'furnishingland', 'interiordesign', 'malfunctioning', 'moisturization', 'multifunctions', 'pscychological', 'rajasthanikart', 'responsibility', 'scientifically', 'sophistication', 'specifications']

for regex and wp tokenizers this list is the same

Notice "brilliantcolor", "furnishingland", "interiordesign", "multifunctions". These are invented words or typos. "rajasthanikart" may be a name of a card specific to India.

In [180]:
# sorted(w for w in vocab_w if w.endswith('ness'))
# sorted(w for w in vocab_regex if len(w) > 6 and fdist_regex[w] > 7)
# sorted(term for term in vocab_w if 'rs' in term)
# sorted(item for item in vocab_w if item.istitle())
# sorted(wd for wd in vocab_w if wd.istitle() and len(wd) > 10)
# sorted(w for w in vocab4 if not w.islower())
# sorted(w for w in vocab_w if '.' in w and 'rs' in w)
# sorted(item for item in vocab_w if item.isdigit())

These are usefull collections. We can take the vocabulary and delete the digits. Or take only the tokens composed of letters, as I did above.

In [181]:
print('number of words', len(test_w), len(test_regex), len(test_wp))
print('vocab size', len(set(test_w)), len(set(test_regex)), len(set(test_wp)))

# print(len(set(word.lower() for word in test_w))) # no need if I did .lower before
# print(len(set(word.lower() for word in test_regex))) # no need if I did .lower before
# print(len(set(word.lower() for word in test_wp))) # no need if I did .lower before

number of words 58894 58949 58949
vocab size 4866 4876 4876

In these conditions of pre-treatment, Wordpunct Tokenizer do the same thing as Regex tokenizer with chosen regular expression. So for next tests I will keep only simple word tokenizer and wordpunct tokenizer.

Prepare dataset¶

Tokenization, keep letters only and long words only, remove stopwords¶

In [182]:
my_stopwords = stopwords.words('english')

'''Meaningless words, specific to this documents set, as "price", "buy" etc, can be thrown away 
with the parameter max_df in vectorizer, be that Count or Tfidf.'''


# my_stopwords.extend(['prices', 'price', 
# 'delivering', 'delivered', 'delivery', 'deliver', 
# 'products', 'product', 'customers', 'customer', 'shopping', 'shops', 'shop', 
#                      'sales', 'sale', 
# #                      'model', 'details'
# #                     'specifications', 'general', 'features',
#                         'online', 'quality', 'buy', 'only'])
print(my_stopwords)

['i', 'me', 'my', 'myself', 'we', 'our', 'ours', 'ourselves', 'you', "you're", "you've", "you'll", "you'd", 'your', 'yours', 'yourself', 'yourselves', 'he', 'him', 'his', 'himself', 'she', "she's", 'her', 'hers', 'herself', 'it', "it's", 'its', 'itself', 'they', 'them', 'their', 'theirs', 'themselves', 'what', 'which', 'who', 'whom', 'this', 'that', "that'll", 'these', 'those', 'am', 'is', 'are', 'was', 'were', 'be', 'been', 'being', 'have', 'has', 'had', 'having', 'do', 'does', 'did', 'doing', 'a', 'an', 'the', 'and', 'but', 'if', 'or', 'because', 'as', 'until', 'while', 'of', 'at', 'by', 'for', 'with', 'about', 'against', 'between', 'into', 'through', 'during', 'before', 'after', 'above', 'below', 'to', 'from', 'up', 'down', 'in', 'out', 'on', 'off', 'over', 'under', 'again', 'further', 'then', 'once', 'here', 'there', 'when', 'where', 'why', 'how', 'all', 'any', 'both', 'each', 'few', 'more', 'most', 'other', 'some', 'such', 'no', 'nor', 'not', 'only', 'own', 'same', 'so', 'than', 'too', 'very', 's', 't', 'can', 'will', 'just', 'don', "don't", 'should', "should've", 'now', 'd', 'll', 'm', 'o', 're', 've', 'y', 'ain', 'aren', "aren't", 'couldn', "couldn't", 'didn', "didn't", 'doesn', "doesn't", 'hadn', "hadn't", 'hasn', "hasn't", 'haven', "haven't", 'isn', "isn't", 'ma', 'mightn', "mightn't", 'mustn', "mustn't", 'needn', "needn't", 'shan', "shan't", 'shouldn', "shouldn't", 'wasn', "wasn't", 'weren', "weren't", 'won', "won't", 'wouldn', "wouldn't"]
In [183]:
def letters_only (my_series):
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word.isalpha()])

def long_only (my_series):
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if len(word) > 2])

def no_stopwords (my_series):
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word not in my_stopwords])

def letters_long_stopwords (my_series):
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word.isalpha() and len(word) > 2 and word not in my_stopwords])

def letters_long (my_series):
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word.isalpha() and len(word) > 2])
In [184]:
descri['token'] = descri.description.map(lambda x: nltk.word_tokenize(x))
# descri['wp_token'] = descri.description.apply(lambda x: wordpunct.tokenize(x))
# descri['regex_token'] = descri.description.apply(lambda x: regexp.tokenize(x))

(descri.token == descri.wp_token).all() # output is False after tokenization.

In [185]:
'''stopwords can be thrown away with parameter "stopwords" of a vectorizer, be that Count or Tfidf"'''
# descri['token'] = letters_only(descri.token)
# descri['wp_token'] = letters_only(descri.wp_token)

# descri['token'] = long_only(descri.token)
# descri['wp_token'] = long_only(descri.wp_token)

# descri['token'] = no_stopwords(descri.token)
# descri['wp_token'] = no_stopwords(descri.wp_token)

# descri['token'] = letters_long_stopwords(descri.token)
# descri['wp_token'] = letters_long_stopwords(descri.wp_token)

# descri['token'] = letters_long(descri.token)
# descri['wp_token'] = letters_long(descri.wp_token)
Out[185]:
'stopwords can be thrown away with parameter "stopwords" of a vectorizer, be that Count or Tfidf"'

(descri.token == descri.wp_token).all() # output is True after remove of short words, non-letter words and stopwords.

The result for Rexex Tokenizer is the same: series are different after tokenization, but identical after removal of non-letter words, short words and stopwords

(just to remind, Regex and Wordpunkt gave identical results already after tokenization).

So, no need to use standard tokenizer and Wordpunct Tokenizer: they give identical results. for the future I will keep only standard NLTK tokenizer.

Stemming and lemmatization¶

I will try 3 stemmers: Porter Stemmer, Lancaster Stemmer (which is harcher than Porter) and WordNet Lemmatizer (the last one needs Download the WordNet corpora from NLTK downloader). The stemmers and lemmatizers are taken from https://www.datacamp.com/community/tutorials/stemming-lemmatization-python

And the columns xxx_token stay non-stemmed un non-lemmatized. I want to compare the results with different stemmers/lemmatizers and in their absence.

In [186]:
stem_porter = PorterStemmer()
stem_lancaster=LancasterStemmer()
lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
lemmatizer_multi = WNL()

Porter Stemmer

In [187]:
descri['porter'] = descri.token.map(lambda x: [stem_porter.stem(word) for word in x])
# descri['wp_porter'] = descri.wp_token.map(lambda x: [stem_porter.stem(word) for word in x])
# descri['regex_porter'] = descri.regex_token.map(lambda x: [stem_porter.stem(word) for word in x])

Lancaster Stemmer

In [188]:
descri['lancaster'] = descri.token.map(lambda x: [stem_lancaster.stem(word) for word in x])
# descri['wp_lancaster'] = descri.wp_token.map(lambda x: [stem_lancaster.stem(word) for word in x])
# descri['regex_lancaster'] = descri.regex_token.map(lambda x: [stem_lancaster.stem(word) for word in x])

WordNet Lemmatizer

In [189]:
descri['lemma'] = descri.token.map(lambda x: [lemmatizer.lemmatize(word, pos='v') for word in x]) # part of speech = verb?
# descri['wp_lemma'] = descri.wp_token.map(lambda x: [w_lemmatizer.lemmatize(word, pos='v') for word in x]) # part of speech = verb?
# descri['regex_lemma'] = descri.regex_token.map(lambda x: [w_lemmatizer.lemmatize(word, pos='v') for word in x]) # part of speech = verb?

# if problems with lemmatization to the needed POS, see
# https://stackoverflow.com/questions/15586721/wordnet-lemmatization-and-pos-tagging-in-python

WordNet lemmatizer with Part-Of-Speech distinction. Recognizes Noun, Verb, Adjective, Adverb (but not Satellite Adjectives)

In [190]:
tag_map = defaultdict(lambda : wordnet.NOUN)
tag_map['J'] = wordnet.ADJ
tag_map['V'] = wordnet.VERB
tag_map['R'] = wordnet.ADV
print(tag_map)
descri['lemma_pos'] = descri.token.map(lambda x: [lemmatizer_multi.lemmatize(token, tag_map[tag[0]]) for token, tag in nltk.pos_tag(x)])
print(tag_map)

defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x000002670925A940>, {'J': 'a', 'V': 'v', 'R': 'r'})
defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x000002670925A940>, {'J': 'a', 'V': 'v', 'R': 'r', 'I': 'n', 'N': 'n', '(': 'n', 'C': 'n', ')': 'n', '$': 'n', 'D': 'n', 'P': 'n', 'T': 'n', 'W': 'n', 'M': 'n', '.': 'n', "'": 'n', 'U': 'n', 'F': 'n', '`': 'n', ':': 'n', 'S': 'n', 'E': 'n', '#': 'n'})
In [191]:
for stem_lemma1 in ['token', 'porter', 'lancaster', 'lemma', 'lemma_pos']:
    for stem_lemma2 in ['token', 'porter', 'lancaster', 'lemma', 'lemma_pos']:
        print((descri[stem_lemma1] == descri[stem_lemma2]).all(), end='\t')
    print()

True	False	False	False	False	
False	True	False	False	False	
False	False	True	False	False	
False	False	False	True	False	
False	False	False	False	True

Different stemmers/lemmatizers, all give non-identical results, unlike different tokenizers. So, it makes sense to keep all the stemmers and lemmatizers, but not the 3 different tokenizers that I tested.

Delete the words that are too rare¶

"If the frequent words don't help us, how about the words that occur once only, the so-called hapaxes? View them by typing fdist1.hapaxes(). This list contains lexicographer, cetological, contraband, expostulations, and about 9,000 others. It seems that there are too many rare words, and without seeing the context we probably can't guess what half of the hapaxes mean in any case! Since neither frequent nor infrequent words help, we need to try something else."

In fact, function hapaxes shows the words that occur only once. To remove it, I have to check every word in database if it is in the list of hapaxes. And keep only those which are not in that list.

I can also remove words occuring less than given number of times. See function "remove hapaxes".

In [192]:
def remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent (my_series):
    my_series_total_d = ' '.join(my_series.map(lambda x: ' '.join(x)))
    my_series_total_d = my_series_total_d.split()
    my_series_total_d = Text(my_series_total_d)
    my_series_fdist = nltk.FreqDist(my_series_total_d)
# this is the same as set min_df=2 in vectorizer, Count or TfIdf
    my_series_hapax = my_series_fdist.hapaxes() # shall I better remove words that occur less than 3 times ? less than 5 times?
#  this is the same as set min_df=3 in vectorizer, Count or TfIdf
#     my_series_lessthan = [w for w in set(my_series_total_d) if my_series_fdist[w] < 3]
    my_series_between = [w for w in set(my_series_total_d) if my_series_fdist[w] < 3 and my_series_fdist[w] < 333]
    print('words number:', len(my_series_total_d), end= '')
    print('; vocab length:', len(my_series_fdist), end= '')
    print('; hapax number:', len(my_series_hapax), end= '')
#     print('; new vocab len:', len(my_series_fdist) - len(my_series_hapax))
#     print('; new vocab len:', len(my_series_lessthan), ';')
    print('; new vocab len:', len(my_series_between), ';')
#     return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word not in my_series_hapax])
#     return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word not in my_series_lessthan])
    return my_series.map(lambda x: [word for word in x if word not in my_series_between])
In [193]:
'''The function "remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent" is eventually not needed, 
as min_df parameter does the same in vectorizer, be that Count of Tfidf.'''

# descri['token'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.token)
# # descri['wp_token'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.wp_token)
# descri['porter'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.porter)
# # descri['wp_porter'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.wp_porter)
# descri['lancaster'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.lancaster)
# # descri['wp_lancaster'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.wp_lancaster)
# descri['lemma'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.lemma)
# # descri['wp_lemma'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.wp_lemma)
# descri['lemma_pos'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.lemma_pos)
# # descri['wp_lemma_pos'] = remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent(descri.wp_lemma_pos)
Out[193]:
'The function "remove_hapaxes_and_too_frequent" is eventually not needed, \nas min_df parameter does the same in vectorizer, be that Count of Tfidf.'

If 'remove_hapaxes" function applied, the results would be:

  • words number: 46077; vocab length: 4777; hapax number: 1407; new vocab len: 3370
  • words number: 46077; vocab length: 3801; hapax number: 1010; new vocab len: 2791
  • words number: 46077; vocab length: 3416; hapax number: 848; new vocab len: 2568
  • words number: 46077; vocab length: 4189; hapax number: 1183; new vocab len: 3006
  • words number: 46077; vocab length: 4165; hapax number: 1193; new vocab len: 2972

Text Pipelines: dimensionality reduction, clustering, evaluation, visualization¶

Dimentionality reduction can be done by two methods: either choose the greedy vectorizer that takes only P most frequent words with parameter max_features of the vectorizer; or takes full vocabulary, re-orient the axes of vocab space using PCA, takes the components (not the words!), in other words the coordinates along new axes, where chosen axes are just the direction of the biggest variance of the dots cloud.

In [194]:
def vectorize_pdseries (myseries, myvectorizer):
#     myse_array = descri[myseries].str.join(' ').values # input string names of a series
    myse_array = myseries.str.join(' ').values # input a series, like df.pdseries
    myse_vectors = myvectorizer.fit_transform(myse_array)
#     print(type(myse_vectors), myse_vectors.shape)
#     return myse_vectors, myvectorizer.get_feature_names()
#     return myse_vectors.toarray(), myvectorizer.get_feature_names()
    return myse_vectors, myse_vectors.toarray()
In [195]:
metric_comparison = []

Dimensionality reduction made with greedy vectorizers¶

CountVectorizer is a part of TF-IDF vectorizer. Tfidf Vectorizer is "Equivalent to CountVectorizer followed by TfidfTransformer". https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer.html

I use the guide from https://towardsdatascience.com/getting-started-with-text-vectorization-2f2efbec6685

In [196]:
# binary_vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
#     binary=True, norm=False,
#     use_idf=False, smooth_idf=False,
#     lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
# #     min_df=2, max_df=0.7, max_features=250, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
# )

# stably gives worse results than other configurations of vectorizer, so it's taken out of comparison
In [197]:
# bag_of_words_vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
#     binary=False, norm=None,  # if vectorizer works badly, change norm None ->False as in binary_vecto
#     use_idf=False, smooth_idf=False,
#     lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
# #     min_df=2, max_df=0.7, max_features=250, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
# )

# # as I understood, in this configuration of parameters TfidfVectorizer is equivalent to CountVectorizer
# # the experiment on a tiny doc set that I generated confirmed it. So I just use CountVectorizer instead
# # just note that TfidfVectorizer always return floats, and here too, while CountVectorizer returns integers
In [198]:
greedy_count = CountVectorizer(min_df=2, max_df=0.29, max_features=400)
In [199]:
greedy_tf  = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l1',
    use_idf=False, smooth_idf=False,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=2, max_df=0.29, max_features=400, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
)

# this one is different from CountVecto in a span of 1 doc and also between the docs. default config of TfidfVecto
In [200]:
greedy_tf_idf = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l2',
    use_idf=True, smooth_idf=True,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=2, max_df=0.29, max_features=400, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
)

# this one is different from CountVecto in a span of 1 doc and also between the docs. default config of TfidfVecto
In [201]:
greedy_tf_idf_bigrams = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l2',
    use_idf=True, smooth_idf=True,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=2, max_df=0.29, max_features=400, ngram_range=(1,2) # default values
)

# this one is different from CountVecto in a span of 1 doc and also between the docs. default config of TfidfVecto
In [202]:
# redu_count_spa, redu_count_arr = vectorize_pdseries(greedy_count_vectorizer, descri.lemma_pos)
# redu_tf_spa, redu_tf_arr = vectorize_pdseries(greedy_tf, descri.lemma_pos)
# redu_tfidf_spa, redu_arr = vectorize_pdseries(greedy_tf_idf, descri.lemma_pos)

Metrics:¶

Given the knowledge of the ground truth class assignments _labelstrue, Adjusted Rand index is a function that measures the similarity of the two assignments. A higher Adjusted Rand index relates to a model with higher similarity.

If the ground truth labels are not known, Silhouette Coefficient is OK. A higher Silhouette Coefficient score relates to a model with better defined clusters.

If the ground truth labels are not known, the Calinski-Harabasz index is OK. A higher Calinski-Harabasz score relates to a model with better defined clusters.

If the ground truth labels are not known, Davies-Bouldin index is OK. A lower Davies-Bouldin index relates to a model with better separation between the clusters

In [203]:
def greedy_vectorizer_pipeline(name_series, name_vectorizer, myn_clusters=7, comparison=True, pictures=False):
    myseries = descri[name_series]
    myvectorizer = eval(name_vectorizer)
    redu_spa, redu_arr = vectorize_pdseries(myseries, myvectorizer)
    print(redu_arr.shape, end='\t')

# #     if pictures:
# #         # original labels tSNEvis: with yellowbrick library
# #         tsne_vis_orig = TSNEVisualizer(decompose=None, perplexity=20, colors=color_palette('tab20'))
# #         # decompose='svd',  decompose='pca', colors=[plt.cm.Set3(i) for i in range(12)]
# #         # when X = redu_spa and decompose='pca', TypeError: PCA does not support sparse input. Use TruncatedSVD
# #         tsne_vis_orig.fit(redu_spa, orig_labels_names) # redu_spa for SVD or None decomposition method; redu_arr for PCA or None decomposition method
# #         tsne_vis_orig.show()

    if pictures:
        # original labels tSNEvis: with tSNE manifold and seaborn graph
        orig_tsne = manifold.TSNE(n_components=2, init='random', perplexity=20, learning_rate='auto') # init='pca' requires redu_arr
        X_trans = orig_tsne.fit_transform(redu_spa)  # init='pca' requires redu_arr
        X_df = pd.DataFrame(X_trans, columns=['dim1', 'dim2'])
        plt.figure()
        ax1 = scatterplot(x='dim1', y='dim2', data=X_df, hue=orig_labels_names, palette='tab10', alpha=0.5)


    # clustering with KMeans on text array
    myclust = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=myn_clusters)
    myclust.fit(redu_spa) # also works with redu_arr, with the same result
    kmeans_labels = myclust.labels_
    if pictures:
        print('Cluster sizes \n{}'.format(pd.Series(kmeans_labels).value_counts())) # someties can be more than a half of all documents in 1 cluster
    
# #     # clustering with GaussianMixture on text array
#     mygaussian = GaussianMixture(n_components=myn_clusters)
#     mygaussian.fit(redu_arr)
# #     gaussian_labels = gaussian.predict(redu_spa) if tests show that Gaussian is better than KMeans
#     kmeans_labels = mygaussian.predict(redu_arr)
#     if pictures:
#         print('Cluster sizes \n{}'.format(pd.Series(kmeans_labels).value_counts())) # someties can be more than a half of all documents in 1 cluster
    
    
    
    # Metrics
    conf_mat = metrics.confusion_matrix(orig_labels, kmeans_labels)
    if comparison:
        metric_comparison.append(['greedy', 'notSNE', name_vectorizer, name_series,
               round(metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.silhouette_score(redu_spa, kmeans_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.davies_bouldin_score(redu_arr, kmeans_labels), 3),  # TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
               round(metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(redu_arr, kmeans_labels), 3)]) # TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.

        
    if pictures:
        print('ARI of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels))
        print('AMutual Info score of KMeans labels', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels))
        print('Homogeneity score of KMeans labels', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels))
        print('Completeness score of KMeans labels', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels))
        print('Fowlkes Mallows score of KMeans labels', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, kmeans_labels))
        print('Silhouette score of KMeans labels', metrics.silhouette_score(redu_spa, kmeans_labels))
        print('Davies-Bouldin index of KMeans labels', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(redu_arr, kmeans_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
        print('Calinski-Harabasz score of KMeans labels', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(redu_arr, kmeans_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
# #       print(conf_mat)
# #       print(metrics.classification_report(orig_labels, my_labels))


# #     # KMeans labels tSNEvis: with yellowbrick library
# #     tsne_vis_kmeans = TSNEVisualizer(decompose=None, perplexity=20, colors=color_palette('tab20'))
# #     # decompose='svd',  decompose='pca', colors=[plt.cm.Set3(i) for i in range(12)]
# #     tsne_vis_kmeans.fit(redu_spa, my_labels) # redu_spa for SVD or None decomposition method; redu_arr # for use PCA or None decomposition method
# #     tsne_vis_kmeans.show()
    
    # KMeans labels tSNEvis: with tSNE manifold and seaborn graph
    myclust_tsne = manifold.TSNE(n_components=2, init='random', perplexity=20, learning_rate='auto') # init='pca' requires redu_arr
#     method='exact'
    X_trans = myclust_tsne.fit_transform(redu_spa) # init='pca' requires redu_arr
    X_df = pd.DataFrame(X_trans, columns=['dim1', 'dim2'])
    if pictures:
        plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
        ax2 = scatterplot(x='dim1', y='dim2', data=X_df, hue=kmeans_labels, palette='tab10', alpha=0.5)
        plt.title('tSNEvis of KMeans clusters') #, fontsize = 30, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
        plt.show()


    # draw a confusion matrix
    if pictures:
        df_cm = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat, index = [label for label in labels_names]) 
        plt.figure()
        ax3 = sns.heatmap(df_cm, annot=True, cmap='Blues', fmt='g')
        plt.show()

    
    # clustering with KMeans on tSNE projection
    cls_from_tsne = cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=7)
    cls_from_tsne.fit(X_trans)
    tsne_labels = cls_from_tsne.labels_
    if pictures:
        print('Cluster sizes \n{}'.format(pd.Series(tsne_labels).value_counts())) # someties can be more than a half of all documents in 1 cluster
    
    
#     #     # clustering with GaussianMixture on text array
#     gaussian_from_tsne = GaussianMixture(n_components=myn_clusters)
#     gaussian_from_tsne.fit(X_trans)
# #     gaussian_tsne_labels = gaussian.predict(redu_spa) if tests show that Gaussian is better than KMeans
#     tsne_labels = gaussian_from_tsne.predict(X_trans)
#     if pictures:
#         print('Cluster sizes \n{}'.format(pd.Series(tsne_labels).value_counts())) # someties can be more than a half of all documents in 1 cluster


    # Metrics
    conf_mat = metrics.confusion_matrix(orig_labels, tsne_labels)
    if comparison:
        metric_comparison.append(['greedy', 'tSNE', name_vectorizer, name_series,
               round(metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.silhouette_score(redu_spa, tsne_labels), 3),
               round(metrics.davies_bouldin_score(redu_arr, tsne_labels), 3),  # TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
               round(metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(redu_arr, tsne_labels), 3)]) # TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
    if pictures:
        print('ARI of labels from tSNE', metrics.adjusted_rand_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels))
        print('AMutual Info score of labels from tSNE', metrics.adjusted_mutual_info_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels))
        print('Homogeneity score of labels from tSNE', metrics.homogeneity_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels))
        print('Completeness score of labels from tSNE', metrics.completeness_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels))
        print('Fowlkes Mallows score of labels from tSNE', metrics.fowlkes_mallows_score(orig_labels, tsne_labels))
        print('Silhouette score of labels from tSNE', metrics.silhouette_score(redu_spa, tsne_labels))
        print('Davies-Bouldin index of labels from tSNE', metrics.davies_bouldin_score(redu_arr, tsne_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
        print('Calinski-Harabasz score of labels from tSNE', metrics.calinski_harabasz_score(redu_arr, tsne_labels)) # when use redu_spa, TypeError: A sparse matrix was passed, but dense data is required. Use X.toarray() to convert to a dense numpy array.
# #       print(conf_mat)
# #       print(metrics.classification_report(orig_labels, my_labels))


#   tSNEvis of clusters BASED ON tSNE PROJECTION
    if pictures:
        plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
        ax4 = sns.scatterplot(x='dim1', y='dim2', data=X_df, hue=tsne_labels, palette='tab10', alpha=0.5)
    #     hue='cl_lab'
        plt.title('TSNEvis of clusters based on tSNE') #, fontsize = 30, pad = 35, fontweight = 'bold')
    #     plt.xlabel('tsne1', fontsize = 26, fontweight = 'bold')
    #     plt.ylabel('tsne2', fontsize = 26, fontweight = 'bold')
    #     plt.legend(prop={'size': 14}) 
        plt.show()

    # draw a confusion matrix
    if pictures:
        df_cm = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat, index = [label for label in labels_names]) 
        plt.figure()
        ax3 = sns.heatmap(df_cm, annot=True, cmap='Blues', fmt='g')
        plt.show()
    return None
In [204]:
# for vectoriz in ['greedy_count_vectorizer', 'greedy_tf', 'greedy_tf_idf', 'greedy_tf_idf_bigrams']:
for vectoriz in ['greedy_count', 'greedy_tf', 'greedy_tf_idf']: # bigrams isn't in the list of champions, not for any metric
#     for stem_lemma in descri.columns[8:]:
    for stem_lemma in ['token', 'porter', 'lancaster', 'lemma', 'lemma_pos']:
        greedy_vectorizer_pipeline(stem_lemma, vectoriz)
# print(descri.columns[6:])    

(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)	(1050, 400)

Metrics:¶

Given the knowledge of the ground truth class assignments _labelstrue, Adjusted Rand index is a function that measures the similarity of the two assignments. A higher Adjusted Rand index relates to a model with higher similarity.

Same for other ground truth demanding scores: higher value is better.

If the ground truth labels are not known, Silhouette Coefficient is OK. A higher Silhouette Coefficient score relates to a model with better defined clusters.

If the ground truth labels are not known, Davies-Bouldin index is OK. A lower Davies-Bouldin index relates to a model with better separation between the clusters

If the ground truth labels are not known, the Calinski-Harabasz index is OK. A higher Calinski-Harabasz score relates to a model with better defined clusters.

In [205]:
rating_greedy = pd.DataFrame(metric_comparison, columns=['Reduction', 'Projection', 'Vectorizer', 'StemLem', 
        'ARI', 'AMII', 'Homo', 'Comple', 'Fowlkes', 'Silho', 'Bouldin', 'Harabasz'])

rating_greedy.Bouldin = rating_greedy.Bouldin * -1
In [206]:
for metric in rating_greedy.columns[4:]:
    print(metric, end=' ')
    display(rating_greedy.sort_values(by=metric, ascending=False).head(2))

Reduction Projection Vectorizer StemLem ARI AMII Homo Comple Fowlkes Silho Bouldin Harabasz

Reduction Projection Vectorizer StemLem ARI AMII Homo Comple Fowlkes Silho Bouldin Harabasz
16 greedy notSNE greedy_tf lemma 0.150 0.413 0.354 0.514 0.369 0.238 -1.981 128.697
18 greedy notSNE greedy_tf lemma_pos 0.135 0.424 0.338 0.595 0.390 0.240 -1.152 122.630

The champions are (they do vary from launch to launch):

  • porter tfidf notSNE (ARI, Homo, Fowlkes),
  • token tf tSNE (ARI)
  • token tfidf notSNE (AMII)
  • lemma_pos tfidf notSNE (Comple)
  • lemma count_vec notSNE(Silhouette)
  • porter tf notSNE (Bouldin, Harabasz)

The highest ARI score I ever sawin this test was 0.59 (for porter stemmer, tf_idf_vectorizer. But there is no evidence taht I can't get higher score with another stemmer or another vectorizer, or both changed, or both unchanged just with a new launch.) For the majority of launches I obtain ARI score 0.47-0.56

Dimensionality reduction made with PCA¶

In [208]:
count_vectorizer = CountVectorizer(min_df=0.01, max_df=0.29, max_features=None)
In [209]:
tf_l1 = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l1',
    use_idf=False, smooth_idf=False,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=5, max_df=0.33, max_features=None, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
)

# this one is identical to CountVectorizer IN THE SPAN of 1 document
# but not BETWEEN DIFFERENT DOCUMENTS
# it's proportional to CountVectorier for any given document
# but what is the same number in different docs for CountVecto, is not for this configu of TfidfVecto
In [210]:
tfidf_l2 = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l2',
    use_idf=True, smooth_idf=True,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=5, max_df=0.29, max_features=None, ngram_range=(1,1) # default values
)

# this one is different from CountVecto in a span of 1 doc and also between the docs. default config of TfidfVecto
In [211]:
tfidf_l2_bigrams = TfidfVectorizer(
    binary=False, norm='l2',
    use_idf=True, smooth_idf=True,
    lowercase=False, # stop_words='english', # don't need to apply these params
    min_df=5, max_df=0.29, max_features=None, ngram_range=(1,2) # default values
)

# this one is different from CountVecto in a span of 1 doc and also between the docs. default config of TfidfVecto
In [212]:
